text: Modern protest movements, from the Arab Spring to the waves of unrest we are seeing in Europe and Russia, are described as “Twitter revolutions” in reference to the important part that social media and global news coverage plays in the reactions of other countries to events unfolding abroad. The Media of Revolt explores the early modern era’s first “Twitter revolution”, when print media allowed both contemporaneous and distant events to be rapidly transmitted and discussed throughout wide stretches of Europe and beyond. Modern historians have long recognized that print played an important part in rebellions during the early modern era, turning what had previously been local uprisings into international events and transforming them into full blown propaganda wars as the rebels sought to gain the support of remote authorities and distant populations. Yet the potential of print has never been fully explored in relation to rebellions and revolts in general. Print allowed differing views on the causes of domestic unrest to be discussed and disseminated at home. However, the authorities always sought to ban critical works on the subject if they were considered to present seditious views, to maintain public order and to prevent the rebellion from receiving foreign aid. It was foreign authors who could tell it like it was, unencumbered by the prejudices and nationalism that limited the discourse of contemporary domestic commentators. The Media of Revolt identifies and analyses these representations in the early modern media and asks how the transnational gaze that they represented such rebellions functioned. In doing so, it reflects on the ways in which these representations impacted upon the historical and political truth of the events themselves.
explanation: Polished, domain-specific prose typical of a scholarly book blurb; it reads like human-authored promotional text with plausible references, though a well-trained AI could also generate it.
